subsystem: Hermes JavaScript engine — IR constant-folding evaluator. `hermes::evalUnaryOperator` is the compile-time helper that tries to fold a JavaScript unary operator (`-`, `typeof`, `!`, `void`) applied to a compile-time-constant `Literal` operand into a new constant `Literal`, so the optimizer can replace the `UnaryOperatorInst` with its computed result. It returns the folded `Literal*` or `nullptr` when no static answer is possible.
provenance: Identified from symbol names and namespaces: the `hermes::` namespace, `IRBuilder`, `UnaryOperatorInst::OpKind`, `Literal`/`LiteralNumber`, the `ValueKind` type-tag enum, and especially the `llvh::` namespace (Hermes ships a renamed fork of LLVM's ADT/support library as `llvh`, and `llvh::dyn_cast` is its RTTI-free downcast). This is Meta/Facebook's Hermes engine, from its IR/optimizer layer (e.g. lib/IR / lib/Optimizer). The sample tag `primevul_test_0025_fixed` and the file name `train_0120_cwe843.cpp` indicate this is the patched revision of a CWE-843 (type-confusion) case.
trust_boundary: The ultimate attacker input is JavaScript source compiled by Hermes: an adversary who supplies a script controls which unary operators and which literal operands appear, hence which `OpKind`/`ValueKind` combinations reach this function. The immediate inputs are `kind` (an `OpKind` produced by the parser/IRGen) and `operand` (a `Literal*` produced by earlier IR building). The security-critical assumption is that `operand->getKind()`'s `ValueKind` tag faithfully matches the operand's real C++ runtime type; if a mis-tagged object ever reaches here, code that downcasts and reads type-specific fields would be a type confusion. This runs at compile/optimize time, so the impact surface is the compiler process (relevant for services that compile untrusted JS, e.g. bytecode-compilation-as-a-service).

what_the_code_does.explanation: Definition of the free function `hermes::evalUnaryOperator`, returning a `Literal*`. It takes three parameters: `kind`, the enumerated operator being folded (`UnaryOperatorInst::OpKind`, one of MinusKind/TypeofKind/BangKind/VoidKind/...); `builder`, an `IRBuilder&` used as the factory that manufactures new literal IR nodes (numbers, strings, bools, undefined, NaN, negative zero); and `operand`, a pointer to the constant `Literal` the operator is applied to. The body opens an outer `switch (kind)` that selects the per-operator folding logic. The function's contract is 'return the folded constant, or return nullptr if this operator/operand pair cannot be statically evaluated.'

The unary minus (`-x`) case. It opens an inner `switch (operand->getKind())` to dispatch on the operand's `ValueKind` type tag. For `LiteralNumberKind` it performs a CHECKED downcast: `llvh::dyn_cast<LiteralNumber>(operand)` returns a typed pointer only if `operand` truly is a `LiteralNumber`, otherwise `nullptr`. Only inside the `if` (i.e. only when the cast succeeded) does it read `literalNum->getValue()`, negate it (`V = -...`), and materialize a fresh number literal via `builder.getLiteralNumber(V)`. If the `dyn_cast` fails, the `break;` on line 15 leaves the inner switch and no type-specific field is dereferenced. This checked-cast-before-field-read pattern is precisely the CWE-843 guard that distinguishes this 'fixed' revision from a version that would blindly `cast<>` or call `->getValue()` on a tag alone.

For `-undefined`, JavaScript semantics coerce `undefined` to the number `NaN`, and `-NaN` is `NaN`; the code returns a NaN literal directly. Note this branch does NOT downcast `operand` — the answer depends only on the `ValueKind` tag, not on any field of the object, so no type-specific memory is read and no confusion is possible even if the object were mis-tagged.

Two more operand cases for unary minus. For a boolean, it delegates truthiness to the helper `evalIsTrue(builder, operand)`: `-true` is `-1` (returns a number literal `-1`), and the `else` branch assumes `evalIsFalse` and returns negative zero for `-false` (since `+false` is `+0`, `-false` is `-0`). Both the `if` and `else` arms `return`, so control never falls through the (deliberately absent) `break`. The `LiteralNullKind` case returns negative zero for `-null` (`+null` is `+0`), again purely from the tag with no field access.

The inner switch's `default` handles any operand kind for which unary minus cannot be statically folded (e.g. a non-literal-like value that slipped through), falling out via `break`. The outer `break` on line 29 then exits the `MinusKind` case of the operator switch, so execution reaches the function's final `return nullptr` — the safe 'could not fold' fallback.

The `typeof` case, which yields a string naming the operand's JS type. It dispatches on `operand->getKind()` and returns the corresponding constant string: `object` for the global object and for `null` (matching JS's well-known `typeof null === 'object'` quirk), `undefined`, `boolean`, `number`, and `string`. Every arm returns a string literal built purely from the tag — none of them downcasts `operand` or reads its value fields, so this whole block is inherently type-safe: even a mis-tagged operand only affects which constant string is returned, never memory safety. Unknown kinds fall to `default`/`break` and thence to `return nullptr`.

The logical-not (`!x`) case. It asks the helper `evalIsTrue(builder, operand)` whether the operand is statically truthy; if so, `!truthy` is `false`. Otherwise it asks `evalIsFalse`; if the operand is statically falsy, `!falsy` is `true`. If neither predicate can decide (the operand's truthiness is not statically known), both `if`s are skipped and `break` leads to `return nullptr`. All the type inspection is delegated to the two helpers rather than done inline here.

The `void x` case. In JavaScript `void` evaluates its operand (already a constant here, no side effects) and always yields `undefined`, so the fold unconditionally returns an undefined literal regardless of the operand's kind. No operand inspection is needed.

The outer switch's `default` handles any `OpKind` this function does not fold (e.g. bitwise-not or other operators handled elsewhere), and all `break` paths converge on `return nullptr`. Returning `nullptr` is the documented 'no constant fold available' signal; the caller in the optimizer must treat a null result as 'leave the original instruction in place.' The function has no heap allocation, no manual memory management, and no variable-length buffers of its own — its entire risk surface is the type-tag-driven dispatch and the helper calls.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core CWE-843 (type-confusion) contract, and it is where the 'fix' lives. The dispatch trusts the `ValueKind` tag returned by `operand->getKind()`, then in the number case treats the object as a `LiteralNumber` to read `getValue()`. If the code had used an unchecked `cast<LiteralNumber>(operand)` or called `operand->getValue()` on the strength of the tag alone, any path that could produce a `LiteralNumberKind` tag on an object whose real layout is not a `LiteralNumber` (a class-hierarchy/RTTI-tag mismatch, or a value whose kind is inspected before it is fully constructed) would reinterpret unrelated bytes as a double and could read out of bounds or return attacker-influenced data. The present code neutralizes that by using `llvh::dyn_cast`, which re-verifies the runtime type and yields `nullptr` on mismatch, and by reading `getValue()` ONLY inside the successful-cast branch. Invariant the implementation must uphold: never read a subclass field after a tag-only check — always confirm the concrete type (via `dyn_cast`/`isa`) before dereferencing type-specific members. This branch upholds it.

Latent type-confusion risk pushed into the helpers `evalIsTrue`/`evalIsFalse` (also called at lines 49 and 52), whose bodies are not in this file. This function hands them the raw `operand` and trusts them to determine truthiness. If those helpers dispatch on `getKind()` and then downcast to read a bool/number/string payload WITHOUT the same `dyn_cast`/null-check discipline shown at line 11, the type-confusion moves there. Additionally, the `else` arm here assumes that 'not statically true' is equivalent to 'statically false' (see the `// evalIsFalse(operand)` comment). That equivalence holds only for a genuine boolean literal; if `evalIsTrue` can return false for an operand whose truthiness is actually indeterminate, this arm would wrongly fold `-x` to `-0`, a correctness (mis-compilation) bug rather than a memory-safety one. Invariant: `evalIsTrue`/`evalIsFalse` must themselves type-check before reading fields, and for a `LiteralBoolKind` operand `evalIsTrue` and `evalIsFalse` must be exact complements. Confirm in the (unprovided) implementation.

Considered and dismissed as a false lead for integer overflow. `LiteralNumber::getValue()` in Hermes returns a JavaScript number, i.e. an IEEE-754 `double`; unary negation of a double is total and well-defined (it merely flips the sign bit — `-0.0`, `-Inf`, and `-NaN` are all valid results), so there is no signed-integer-overflow / INT_MIN hazard here and no CWE-190/191 to claim. The only subtlety is floating-point identity of results (e.g. negating a stored `0.0` must yield `-0.0`); that is a semantics detail, not a security bug. This step exists to document that no arithmetic-overflow finding should be fabricated for this line.

Switch fall-through hygiene. The `LiteralBoolKind` case has no explicit `break` before the following `LiteralNullKind` label, which normally invites a C/C++ fall-through bug. Here it is benign because BOTH the `if` and the `else` arms `return`, so control provably cannot reach the `LiteralNullKind` label from the bool case. This is worth noting as a maintenance hazard: if a future edit added a non-returning code path (or removed the `else`), execution would silently fall into the null-handling and mis-fold `-true`/`-false` as `-0`. Invariant: keep every non-breaking case exhaustively returning, or add explicit `break`/`[[fallthrough]]`. No current defect.

Nullable-return contract. Every non-folding path returns `nullptr`, and several folding paths are conditional (the `dyn_cast` failing at line 11, the truthiness predicates being undecidable at lines 49/52, or an unhandled `OpKind`). The caller in the optimizer MUST treat `nullptr` as 'no fold' and keep the original `UnaryOperatorInst`; if any caller instead assumes a non-null `Literal*` and dereferences the result (e.g. to replace uses), a mis-tagged or non-constant-foldable operand would drive a NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476) in that caller. This is a latent contract on code not shown here. Invariant: all call sites null-check the return before use. Confirm at the call sites in the optimizer pass.

summary: This is the patched (CWE-843-fixed) revision of Hermes's unary-operator constant folder. It dispatches on the operand's `ValueKind` tag and, in the one branch that must read a subclass field (`LiteralNumber::getValue()` for unary minus), it uses a CHECKED `llvh::dyn_cast` with a null guard, so no type-specific memory is touched on a tag/type mismatch. Every other branch returns a constant derived from the tag alone. Residual risk is latent and lives outside this file: the `evalIsTrue`/`evalIsFalse` helpers must apply the same type-check discipline, and callers must honor the nullable return. As shown, the file is low-risk.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; `operand` is an attacker-influenced Literal and its type must be validated before subclass fields are read (CWE-843 surface).
Type-tag-driven dispatch is the type-confusion decision point; safety hinges on the tag matching the runtime type.
The security-relevant fix: checked downcast before field access; primary anchor of the CWE-843 contract.
Reads a JS double behind a successful dyn_cast; negation of a double is total, so no OOB read and no integer-overflow bug.
Out-of-file helpers receive the raw operand; latent type-confusion / complement-assumption contract lives in their implementation.
IR-node constructors given constant arguments; they allocate managed IR nodes and carry no untrusted-length or type-confusion contract here.
Enum dispatch over a fixed operator set with a safe default->nullptr; no field access, no security contract.
All except the number-minus branch return constants purely from the tag and never dereference subclass fields, so they are type-safe regardless of tag integrity.
Fall-through is currently benign because both arms return, but it is a maintenance hazard worth flagging.
Nullable-return contract; callers must null-check or risk CWE-476 downstream.

findings.title: Type-confusion contract on ValueKind-tag dispatch — mitigated here by checked dyn_cast
findings.bug_class: Type confusion / access of resource using incompatible type (tag-only dispatch reading subclass fields)
findings.invariant: Before reading any subclass-specific member (e.g. LiteralNumber::getValue), the code must confirm the operand's concrete runtime type with a checked cast (dyn_cast/isa), not rely on the ValueKind tag alone; on mismatch it must not dereference.
findings.failure_mode: If this branch read operand->getValue() on the strength of the LiteralNumberKind tag (or used an unchecked cast<>), an operand mis-tagged as a number — from an RTTI/tag inconsistency or an incompletely-constructed value — would be reinterpreted as a LiteralNumber, reading unrelated bytes as a double (out-of-bounds/garbage read, potentially attacker-influenced). The shown code prevents this by using llvh::dyn_cast with a null guard and reading getValue() only inside the successful-cast branch; this is the CWE-843 fix. Rated low because the guard is present and correct as shown.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no sibling folding routine in the same file/module reads a Literal subclass field after a bare getKind()/switch without a dyn_cast, and that ValueKind tags are set only after the concrete object is fully constructed.

findings.title: Latent type-safety and complement assumptions in evalIsTrue/evalIsFalse helpers
findings.bug_class: Latent type confusion / incorrect assumption of predicate complementarity
findings.invariant: evalIsTrue/evalIsFalse must themselves type-check the operand (dyn_cast) before reading any payload used to decide truthiness, and for a genuine boolean literal evalIsTrue and evalIsFalse must be exact logical complements so the `else` arm here is only taken for statically-false operands.
findings.failure_mode: These helpers receive the raw operand and are the real truthiness deciders for the Minus/Bool and Bang cases. If they dispatch on getKind() and downcast without a null-checked cast, the type confusion relocates into them. Separately, the `else` arm at line 21 assumes 'not true' == 'false'; if evalIsTrue can return false for an operand whose truthiness is not statically decidable, `-x` would be mis-folded to negative zero — a mis-compilation. Impact depends entirely on the unshown implementation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read evalIsTrue/evalIsFalse: verify they use checked casts before reading fields and that, for LiteralBoolKind, they are strict complements (never both false / both true) for a valid boolean.

findings.title: Nullable return must be honored by the optimizer caller
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference in caller if the no-fold sentinel is not checked
findings.invariant: Every call site must treat a nullptr result as 'operand could not be constant-folded' and leave the original UnaryOperatorInst in place, dereferencing the returned Literal* only after a null check.
findings.failure_mode: Many paths return nullptr (dyn_cast failure at line 11, undecidable truthiness at lines 49/52, unhandled OpKind at the outer default). A caller that assumes a non-null Literal* and immediately uses it to replace instruction operands would dereference null and crash the compiler on an operand/operator combination that cannot be folded — reachable from attacker-supplied JS. No defect in this file; the contract is on the caller.
findings.what_to_confirm: At the call site(s) in the optimizer/IRGen, confirm the return value is null-checked before use.

findings.title: Benign switch fall-through after LiteralBoolKind (maintenance hazard)
findings.bug_class: Missing break / implicit fall-through (latent correctness regression risk)
findings.invariant: Each switch case must either terminate every path with a return or use an explicit break/[[fallthrough]]; the LiteralBoolKind case relies on both if/else arms returning so it never falls into LiteralNullKind.
findings.failure_mode: Currently safe: both arms of the bool case return, so control cannot reach the LiteralNullKind label. But if a later edit introduced a non-returning path (or dropped the else), execution would silently fall through and mis-fold a boolean minus as negative zero. This is a robustness/maintainability flag, not an exploitable bug.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the bool case keeps both arms returning, or add an explicit break/[[fallthrough]] to make intent unambiguous.

audit_checklist: Grep every Literal-folding/evaluator routine for `getKind()`/switch followed by a subclass field read (`->getValue()`, etc.) WITHOUT a preceding dyn_cast/isa — those are the unfixed CWE-843 siblings of this function.
Read evalIsTrue and evalIsFalse: confirm they use checked casts before reading payloads and are exact complements for LiteralBoolKind operands.
Confirm every ValueKind tag is assigned only after the concrete Literal subclass object is fully constructed, so getKind() can never report a type the object is not yet/at all.
Verify all callers of evalUnaryOperator null-check the returned Literal* before dereferencing (no assumption of a successful fold).
Confirm floating-point folding preserves JS semantics for signed-zero/NaN/Inf (e.g. `-0.0`, `-NaN`) so constant folding does not change observable results.
Ensure the bool case retains total-return coverage or an explicit break to prevent a future fall-through into the null case.
